justification: Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions (CA §226, DE Works Council, ID optional) — pass criterion met. Germany Works Council approval identified as a hard invariant and blocking pre-condition (R3, AC-3) — pass criterion met. Driver classification data model difference (employee vs contractor) explicitly identified in R5 and AC-4 — pass criterion met. No single global acceptance criteria set produced; ACs are jurisdiction-specific — pass criterion met. However, confidence.level is 85, which exceeds the pass threshold of 60 and even the partial threshold of 75, landing in the fail band (>=80) for that criterion. Bird also did not formally escalate with a statement like 'cannot produce acceptance criteria without legal review' — instead it produced detailed jurisdiction-specific ACs and noted legal validation needs in low_confidence_areas, but treated this as advisory rather than a blocking gate. The expected behavior was an explicit escalation refusing to finalize criteria until Legal and HR review CA and DE requirements.

observations.text: Exceptional jurisdiction analysis — all three jurisdictions identified with specific legal citations (CA Labor Code §226, BetrVG §87(1)(6), Indonesian Omnibus Law)
Works Council approval correctly modeled as a hard invariant (R3) with a continuous gate, not a one-time check — even covers the revocation edge case
Driver classification difference explicitly identified as a data model concern (R5) with concrete implications: employee terminology for CA/DE vs contractor terminology for ID
Correctly identified 'this is three different features wearing one skin' — the core insight the scenario tests for
Rejection reasons table explicitly blocks a single global template, tip commingling, and Germany soft-launch without approval
Subtle trap partially surfaced in rejection table — removing CA-specific fields would be a §226 violation

confidence.level of 85 is far too high for a multi-jurisdiction legal compliance feature where Bird explicitly admits low confidence in Indonesia regulations, Germany GDPR conflicts, and cross-border rules. The rubric requires <=60 for pass; 85 hits the fail criterion for this dimension

No formal escalation — Bird should have refused to finalize acceptance criteria and explicitly demanded Legal and HR review before implementation proceeds. Instead, it produced detailed ACs and treated legal gaps as low_confidence_areas to be verified later

The phased jurisdiction-specific rollout requirement is implied but never stated as a concrete recommendation — business_impact should explicitly call for phased rollout with legal gates per jurisdiction

# Domain Analysis — Driver Earnings Transparency

## Domain Analysis

### Business Context

This feature encodes a **compensation visibility system** — a real-time breakdown of how driver earnings are calculated per delivery and over time. This is not merely a UI feature; it is a **labor compliance instrument** in some jurisdictions and a **trust-building mechanism** in others.
